Basic Damage Waiver (reduces excess to stated amount, not zero)

Super Collision Damage Waiver (SCDW) - reduces excess to zero
Super Theft Protection (STP) - reduces theft excess to zero

rate_inflators_to_identify: SCDW - up to £30.28/day mainland UK: largest individual optional extra. Commonly bundled into credit hire rates - must be stripped out.

Protection packages - up to £52.99/day: Zero Excess and Complete packages include SCDW+STP+Windscreen ± Roadside. If the credit hire rate equates to a protection-bundled rate, defendants should argue only the base DW/TP is recoverable.
Fuel Up Front / Pay on Return: never part of BHR - these are consumable charges.
Additional driver (£14.99/day mainland, £15/day NI): not part of BHR unless the claimant evidences a specific need for a second driver that was satisfied in the original vehicle.
GPS / Travel Companion (up to £14.99/day): not part of BHR absent specific evidence.
Young driver surcharge (£40/day under 25): legitimate add-on where hirer is under 25. Check claimant's age.
Roadside Assistance Plus (up to £5/day mainland, £9.28/day NI): not part of BHR - mechanical breakdown is already covered.
bhr_analysis_notes.what_defendants_argue: Avis's published rate card shows a clear separation between the 'vehicle rental price' (base) and a suite of optional extras that can more than double the day rate. The BHR must be the vehicle-only price. Any credit hire rate that includes SCDW, protection packages, fuel, additional driver or GPS is not a like-for-like comparator.
bhr_analysis_notes.what_claimants_argue: Avis's own product structure anticipates that a rational retail hirer will take DW, TP and (often) SCDW to avoid a £1,500 excess exposure. A 'bare-metal' rate is a fiction: in the real mainstream market no claimant replaces a damaged car with a vehicle carrying £1,500 of personal exposure. Therefore protection-inclusive retail rates are the correct comparator.
bhr_analysis_notes.key_case_law_cross_references: Knowledge base articles 71-72 (Scotland BHR burden of proof); case database entries on Stevens v Equity, McBride v UK Insurance, Bunting v Zurich, Clark v Ardington.

bhr_analysis_notes.key_difference_from_avis: Europcar includes Basic Protection (CDW + TW) in its standard daily rate. When comparing credit hire rates to Europcar's BHR, defendants should note that Europcar's published rate ALREADY includes CDW/TW — so no adjustment is needed for basic damage waiver. However, if the credit hire rate includes zero-excess (Premium) protection, the defendant can still strip out the Premium Protection upgrade cost.
rate_inflators_to_identify: Premium Protection Package (zero-excess): adds £54.50–£80.00/day over the basic rate with Basic Protection — if the credit hire rate effectively provides zero-excess protection, this is the Europcar equivalent cost to strip out
Medium Protection Package: adds £42.50–£73.50/day over basic rate
Young driver surcharge: up to £79.00/day — legitimate if hirer was aged 22–25
Additional driver: £19.10/day — only relevant if need is evidenced
Satellite navigation: £17.84/day — not part of BHR
Fuel charges: pump price x 2.5 penalty — not part of BHR
Premium location surcharge: up to 20% uplift — location-dependent, check if claimant collected near airport/station
Roadside Assistance Plus/Express: up to £20/day — not standard
bhr_analysis_notes.what_defendants_argue: Even though Europcar includes CDW in its base rate, credit hire rates often include zero-excess protection equivalent to Europcar's Premium Protection Package. The defendant is entitled to strip the Premium Protection uplift (£54.50–£80/day) from the credit hire rate for comparison. Additionally, fuel charges, additional drivers, GPS and other extras should be excluded.
bhr_analysis_notes.what_claimants_argue: Europcar's published daily rate already includes CDW and TW as standard. Claimants can argue their credit hire rate is comparable to the Europcar rate (which also bundles protection). The difference in excess cover is a matter of degree, not of kind.
bhr_analysis_notes.key_case_law_cross_references: See knowledge base articles 71-72 (Scotland BHR burden of proof) and case database entries on Stevens v Equity, McBride v UK Insurance, Bunting v Zurich. Note that Europcar is consistently listed in mainstream BHR surveys.

bhr_analysis_notes.key_difference_from_avis_europcar: Thrifty imposes mileage limits on all rentals. For credit hire claimants who drove significant distances, the Thrifty BHR rate is only comparable if mileage is accounted for — excess mileage charges at 15p+VAT/mile would need to be added. A credit hire with unlimited mileage is therefore not directly comparable to Thrifty without adjustment.
rate_inflators_to_identify: Mileage in excess of 100 miles/day (1–14 days): 15p+VAT/mile standard, 50p+VAT/mile Luxury — if claimant drove more than the daily cap, Thrifty equivalent cost would be higher, weakening the BHR argument
DLR (damage liability reduction): £9.50–£20.00/day depending on category and duration — not included in base rate, may be included in credit hire rate
Glass and Tyre Cover: £6.05–£19.80/day — not included in base rate
Young driver surcharge: £35.00/day incl VAT for drivers aged 22–24
Late return charges: daily/hourly rate — not relevant to BHR but evidences pattern of use
Penalty charges (warning light £1,000; missed service £2,000–£10,000) — contractual risk not relevant to BHR rate comparison
bhr_analysis_notes.what_defendants_argue: Thrifty's mileage-limited rates are not directly comparable to credit hire rates that include unlimited mileage. Defendants should ascertain actual mileage driven during the credit hire period and calculate what the Thrifty cost would have been including excess mileage charges. DLR and Glass & Tyre Cover should also be stripped from the credit hire rate for comparison, as these are optional extras under Thrifty T&Cs.
bhr_analysis_notes.what_claimants_argue: Thrifty is a recognised BHR comparator. Its base rate includes insurance and VAT as standard. Claimants can argue that mileage was within the daily cap or that unlimited mileage products from other comparators should be used instead. The 30-day maximum hire period means Thrifty may not be a valid comparator for extended hires.
bhr_analysis_notes.validity_as_comparator_note: Thrifty's 30-day maximum rental period means it cannot be used as a BHR comparator for credit hires exceeding 30 days. Additionally, the mandatory mileage cap is a material structural difference from standard credit hire arrangements.
bhr_analysis_notes.key_case_law_cross_references: See knowledge base articles 71-72 (Scotland BHR burden of proof). Thrifty appears in some regional BHR surveys but its mileage restrictions and 30-day cap are often overlooked by defendants using headline rates. See also Stevens v Equity, McBride v UK Insurance re: rate stripping principles.

versions.changes: Current version as captured March 2026. Child seat charge increased from £5 to £12.91/day. Cash and cheque payments no longer accepted (credit/debit card only). Overseas travel still suspended. Core CDW excess amounts and excess reduction products unchanged from 2022 baseline.

basic_hire_rate_structure.early_return_note: Prepaid tariff: no refund for early return. Pay Later tariff: partial discount for unused days.

damage_excess_gbp_gb.minimum_excess_ldw_age_restriction: NOT available to renters under 25 years in GB

fuel_charges.ni_surcharge_rate: 150% on non-prepaid fuel matrix rate; 10% on prepaid rate

protection_products_gb.loss_damage_waiver: Reduces excess to £0–£150 depending on product/age/vehicle. Standard CDW in base rate has £1,600–£3,000 excess. Minimum excess LDW not available under 25.
protection_products_gb.tyre_and_glass_protection: Waives tyre, windscreen and glass liability to £0 — optional extra
protection_products_gb.roadside_protection: Extended breakdown cover — locked keys, flat battery, fuel, stuck
protection_products_gb.complementary_protection_plus: Waives Sixt's claim for damages up to £500 AND above £30,000 per event — effectively covers the uninsured gap

overseas_use.gb: Zone 1 countries (most of Western Europe) permitted with fee. Zone 2 not permitted. Ferry crossings within Zone 1 permitted.
overseas_use.ni: Republic of Ireland only (with written permission, charge of £24/day max £48/28 days). Leaving island of Ireland incurs £500 fine.

key_contractual_provisions.cdw_included_in_base_rate: CDW is included in the Sixt base rate (clause 4.10 GB T&Cs) — unlike Avis and Thrifty where CDW is a separate extra. However, unlike Europcar's 'Basic Protection', the Sixt CDW excess is high (£1,600–£3,000) and a zero-excess LDW upgrade is available but restricted to over-25s.
key_contractual_provisions.ldw_mandatory_in_ni: Loss Damage Waiver is compulsory for all renters in Northern Ireland under Downeys Rental Ltd t/a Sixt NI T&Cs.
key_contractual_provisions.young_driver_threshold_gb: Young driver surcharge applies to all drivers under 28 in GB — broader threshold than most other mainstream providers (Avis/Europcar: under 25; Thrifty: under 25). Amount not fixed in T&Cs.
key_contractual_provisions.under_25_ldw_restriction_gb: Renters under 25 in GB cannot access the minimum excess (zero-excess) LDW product. They are stuck with the standard £1,600–£3,000 CDW excess.
key_contractual_provisions.mileage_gb: Limited or unlimited depending on tariff — unlike Thrifty (always capped) and similar to Avis/Europcar.
key_contractual_provisions.no_early_return_refund_prepaid: Prepaid tariff: no refund for early return. Pay Later tariff: partial discount available under Flexi Return Guarantee.
key_contractual_provisions.maximum_rental_84_days: Maximum hire period is 84 days — same as Europcar, longer than Thrifty (30 days).
key_contractual_provisions.ni_separate_entity: Sixt NI is operated by Downeys Rental Ltd (NI636209), a franchisee of Sixt GmbH. It is NOT the same company as Sixt Rent a Car Ltd (GB). Different T&Cs, different deposits, different admin fees, and Northern Ireland courts have jurisdiction.
key_contractual_provisions.ni_territorial_restriction: NI vehicles restricted to Northern Ireland unless written permission obtained for Republic of Ireland use.

bhr_analysis_notes.cdw_position_vs_comparators: Sixt sits between Avis/Thrifty (CDW not included in base rate) and Europcar (CDW/TW fully included). Sixt includes CDW in its base rate but with a large excess (£1,600–£3,000), making the base rate comparable to a CDW-included product at a higher excess level. This is a nuanced position for BHR analysis.
rate_inflators_to_identify: Minimum excess LDW (zero-excess upgrade): amount not published in T&Cs — must be obtained from actual Sixt tariff at relevant time and location
Young driver surcharge (under 28 in GB, under 25 in NI): amount not fixed in T&Cs — must be obtained from booking confirmation
Tyre and glass protection: amount not published in T&Cs — must be obtained from tariff
Premium Location Fee: automatic surcharge for airport/train station rentals
Fuel charges: 160% surcharge on missing fuel (GB); 150% (NI) — not part of BHR
Additional driver fee: charged per driver — not standard in base rate
Cleaning charge: £75 if specialist cleaning required
Complementary Protection Plus: waives gap in coverage — not part of base BHR
bhr_analysis_notes.what_defendants_argue: Sixt's base rate includes CDW but with a £1,600–£3,000 excess. If the credit hire rate includes zero-excess protection, the defendant can argue for stripping the cost of the minimum excess LDW upgrade. Young driver surcharges (under 28 in GB) may also be stripped if the claimant was under 28. Premium Location Fees should be stripped if the claimant's need arose away from an airport/station.
bhr_analysis_notes.what_claimants_argue: Sixt's base rate already includes CDW as standard (unlike Avis/Thrifty), so the comparison base is a rate that includes damage cover. The remaining excess gap between Sixt's standard CDW and zero-excess protection is a matter of degree. Claimants may argue the high LDW excess (£1,600–£3,000) makes the base Sixt rate unsuitable for comparison without the LDW upgrade, weakening defendants' strip-out arguments.
bhr_analysis_notes.ni_specific_note: For Northern Ireland credit hire cases, the BHR comparator must use the Downeys Rental Ltd (Sixt NI) tariff, not the Sixt GB tariff. LDW is mandatory in NI, so the base rate already includes zero-excess LDW — this means no LDW strip-out is available when using Sixt NI as the BHR comparator.
bhr_analysis_notes.key_case_law_cross_references: See knowledge base articles 71-72 (Scotland BHR burden of proof) and Stevens v Equity, McBride v UK Insurance, Bunting v Zurich for rate stripping principles. Note that Sixt is commonly used in mainstream BHR surveys — but always check whether the survey rate is for the GB entity or the NI franchisee when handling Northern Ireland claims.

bhr_analysis_notes: CDW (Damage Waiver) and Theft Protection usually included as standard — CRITICAL: must verify from specific Rental Agreement Form. If included, strip both from claimed BHR rate when using Budget as comparator.
Two-contract structure: for BHR challenges the relevant contracting entity is the local rental provider (usually Avis Budget UK Ltd), not the booking agent AB Group Financial Services Ltd.

Sub-operators at specific locations are legally separate entities — verify entity from Rental Agreement Form: Regal Vehicle Rentals (Barrow, Blackpool, Bolton, Carlisle, Chester, Warrington), Hewitt Hire (Cardiff Downtown, Exeter, Newton Abbot, Torquay, Totnes), Polar Cars (Jersey), Flynn Bros (Belfast International and City).

Northern Ireland (Flynn Bros. NI023192): materially different provisions — higher pre-auth excess (£1,700–£2,500 vs mainland UK £300 buffer), higher CDW rates (£10.12/day vs £7.76/day), debit card use requires SCDW purchase, higher fines admin fee (£50 vs £30).
Jersey (Polar Cars): Crown Dependency jurisdiction, not subject to UK or EU consumer law — check jurisdiction-specific rules when using as BHR comparator.
Young driver surcharge: £40/day for under-25s — must strip from BHR if claimant is under 25 and BHR rate includes young driver element.
Excess mileage: 12–21 pence per mile if mileage limit applies — identify from RAF whether limit was applied and whether excess mileage costs are included in claimed BHR.
Early return: no refund policy — full period charged regardless of actual usage. BHR rate therefore validly charged for full period even if vehicle returned early.
Maximum hire period 28 days — after 28 days a new contract is required. Continuous credit hires exceeding 28 days with Budget must have been on successive contracts.
Dartford Crossing auto-payment (£2.50 admin fee) may appear as a charge on credit hire invoices for London-area rentals — legitimate pass-through, not inflated.

basic_hire_rate_structure.notes: The 'vehicle rental price' quoted at point of booking is the base hire rate. All optional extras and add-ons below are chargeable separately and should NOT form part of a BHR comparison unless the claimant can evidence specific need.
what_is_included_in_base_rate: Provision of roadworthy vehicle in stated group
Mandatory third-party liability insurance (UK + Europe where continental cover added)
Basic Damage Waiver (reduces excess to stated amount, not zero)
Basic Theft Protection (reduces theft excess to stated amount, not zero)
Unlimited mileage on most vehicle groups

Congestion charge / clean-air-zone / toll administration fees

damage_waiver_dw.notes: Often included as standard - check Rental Agreement Form at pick-up

super_collision_damage_waiver_scdw.notes: Reduces damage excess to zero (mainland UK) / to £250 on vans. In Northern Ireland forms part of Excess Reduction.

fuel_charges.notes_for_bhr: Pay on Return multipliers significantly inflate apparent rental cost. Fuel is a genuine consumable and not part of BHR.

accepted_uk_cars: American Express (not accepted for Travel Companion Tablet)
rejected: Revolut / neobank / e-wallet style cards (Northern Ireland specifically)

key_contractual_provisions.early_return_note: No refund for unused rental days. Relevant to BHR defence: claimant's insistence on full hire period is contractually rational - early return is a gratuitous forfeiture of consideration.
key_contractual_provisions.vehicle_availability_if_unavailable: Avis will find a replacement in a higher group at no extra cost, or pay the difference if the claimant rents from another company.
prohibited_uses: Commercial hire / hire or reward (unless agreed)
Driving outside permitted countries without prior agreement

accident_reporting_obligations.consequence_of_non_compliance: Loss of damage waiver / theft protection benefits

key_contractual_provisions.governing_law: Law of the country where the vehicle is collected

rate_inflators_to_identify: SCDW - up to £30.28/day mainland UK: largest individual optional extra. Commonly bundled into credit hire rates - must be stripped out.

Protection packages - up to £52.99/day: Zero Excess and Complete packages include SCDW+STP+Windscreen ± Roadside. If the credit hire rate equates to a protection-bundled rate, defendants should argue only the base DW/TP is recoverable.
Fuel Up Front / Pay on Return: never part of BHR - these are consumable charges.
Additional driver (£14.99/day mainland, £15/day NI): not part of BHR unless the claimant evidences a specific need for a second driver that was satisfied in the original vehicle.
GPS / Travel Companion (up to £14.99/day): not part of BHR absent specific evidence.
Young driver surcharge (£40/day under 25): legitimate add-on where hirer is under 25. Check claimant's age.
Roadside Assistance Plus (up to £5/day mainland, £9.28/day NI): not part of BHR - mechanical breakdown is already covered.
bhr_analysis_notes.what_defendants_argue: Avis's published rate card shows a clear separation between the 'vehicle rental price' (base) and a suite of optional extras that can more than double the day rate. The BHR must be the vehicle-only price. Any credit hire rate that includes SCDW, protection packages, fuel, additional driver or GPS is not a like-for-like comparator.
bhr_analysis_notes.what_claimants_argue: Avis's own product structure anticipates that a rational retail hirer will take DW, TP and (often) SCDW to avoid a £1,500 excess exposure. A 'bare-metal' rate is a fiction: in the real mainstream market no claimant replaces a damaged car with a vehicle carrying £1,500 of personal exposure. Therefore protection-inclusive retail rates are the correct comparator.
bhr_analysis_notes.key_case_law_cross_references: Knowledge base articles 81-82 (Budget terms); 71-72 (Scotland BHR burden of proof); case database entries on Stevens v Equity, McBride v UK Insurance, Bunting v Zurich, Clark v Ardington.
bhr_analysis_notes.note_on_avis_equivalence: Budget is operated by Avis Budget UK Limited (company 00802486) — the same entity that operates Avis. The General Conditions and UK Country Conditions are substantively identical. Rates, fees, protection products, fuel policies, and contractual provisions are the same. The only differences are branding and certain sub-operator arrangements at specific locations (see Budget company entry for sub-operators: Regal Vehicle Rentals, Hewitt Hire, Polar Cars, Flynn Bros).

cdw.name: Collision Damage Waiver (CDW)

May be mandatory and included in some contract or preferential rates. If CDW is declined, renter is liable up to the full current replacement value of the vehicle.
cdw.non_waivable_excess_note: A non-waivable excess applies regardless of whether CDW is accepted or included. This excess can only be eliminated by purchasing SuperCover.

theft_protection.name: Theft Protection (TP)
theft_protection.tp_position_detail: TP is 'often included in your rate' — conditional, same position as CDW. If declined, renter liable for full replacement value.

bhr_analysis_notes: CDW and TP are both 'often included' in the rate — the same conditional language as Europcar, Sixt and Budget. Inclusion must be verified on the Rental Agreement, not assumed.
Even where CDW/TP are included, a non-waivable excess persists per incident and can only be eliminated by purchasing SuperCover. This is directly relevant to BHR comparisons with credit hire rates.
Damage Administration Charge of £45 applies per damage event regardless of CDW/TP status. Waived only if SuperCover is purchased.

Young driver surcharge of £61.43/day (max £1,719.98/rental) applies only to groups A/B/C/E/S type vehicles from age 21; the majority of vehicle groups have a strict minimum age of 25 with no surcharge option.
Additional driver fee: £20.60/day with a rental maximum of £206.04 per driver — directly comparable benchmark for BHR rebuttal purposes.
29-minute late return grace period is distinctive — after this, a full new 24-hour period is charged plus a potential additional Late Return Charge.
Longer rental provision: vehicle must be returned every 28 days for service and Rental Agreement renewal — important for extended credit hire claims.

Post-Brexit restriction: UK-rented Hertz vehicles cannot enter any EU country. Travel to Northern Ireland and Republic of Ireland is permitted with express Hertz consent; Republic of Ireland requires VE103B at £171.99-£301.73.

national.notes: National Car Rental is part of Enterprise Holdings (same parent as Enterprise Rent-A-Car and Alamo Rent A Car). In the UK, National primarily targets transatlantic visitors — US and Canadian customers who book their UK rental before travelling. In practice, National UK branches typically share premises, staff and vehicle fleet with the co-located Enterprise and Alamo operations. This means National, Enterprise and Alamo in the UK are effectively the same physical operation trading under three brand names. A BHR survey that cites all three as independent comparators is not producing three separate pieces of market evidence — it is citing the same supplier three times. The 8 FAQ documents available from nationalcar.co.uk reflect primarily US/Canada operational terms with USD pricing. UK-specific GBP rates for young driver surcharges, additional driver fees and LDW are not published in the available documents.

bhr_analysis_notes: CRITICAL — LDW NOT STANDARD: National's LDW is explicitly optional and not included in the rental rate. Unlike Enterprise (DW included as standard in all rates), a National rate quote does not include damage waiver unless separately purchased. Any BHR report quoting National rates must identify whether LDW has been added and at what cost.

CRITICAL — SHARED OPERATION (UK): In the UK, National branches typically share premises, staff and vehicle fleet with co-located Enterprise and Alamo branches. All three are Enterprise Holdings brands. A BHR survey citing National, Enterprise and Alamo as three separate comparators is therefore citing the same physical operation under three brand names — not three independent pieces of market evidence. This is a significant challenge to the independence and reliability of the BHR survey methodology.

UK MARKET FOCUS — TRANSATLANTIC VISITORS: National's UK presence primarily serves US/Canadian customers who pre-book before travelling to the UK. This means National's UK published tariff may reflect a different customer base and pricing strategy (pre-booked transatlantic corporate/leisure travel) compared to domestic spot-market rates. BHR reports quoting National UK rates should be asked to confirm the tariff type and booking conditions used.

DOCUMENTATION GAP — USD RATES: All 8 FAQ documents from nationalcar.co.uk quote rates in USD and reference US state-specific rules. No UK-specific GBP rates for young driver surcharges, additional driver fees or LDW pricing are published in these documents. Any BHR report relying on National UK must disclose the specific UK GBP rate components used.
Minimum rental age is 21 with a Young Renter Fee for ages 21–24. UK GBP amount not published in available documents — must be verified via UK booking engine.

Additional driver fee averages $12/day (US rate). UK GBP equivalent not published. Must be verified. Fee is waived for Emerald Club members' spouses/family at same address and for certain corporate rentals.

Credit card required at rental — debit cards and cash not accepted as deposits. A claimant without a credit card cannot access National regardless of their financial position. Relevant to impecuniosity arguments.
Towing and hitching strictly prohibited. National is not an appropriate BHR comparator for a claimant whose vehicle was used for towing.

Roadside Plus (RSP) is an optional paid product described as available in US and Canada only — not confirmed as available for UK rentals. Any BHR rate incorporating RSP for a UK rental should be challenged.
ECRCS/BVRLA membership status not confirmed from available documents. Should be verified if National rates are being relied upon in GTA/BHR settlement discussions.

Must not be used by any person not licensed and insured to use it

Must not be used if any mechanical, electrical or structural failure or damage where further damage might result
Must not be altered or added to in any way

repairs.notification_obligation: Hirer liable for cost of notification work required as result of unauthorised repairs

AMT administration charges relating to all the above

payment_obligations: Hire charges published in tariff (unless different charges agreed in writing)
All fuel used and any refuelling charge
Any accessories, tyres, tools or equipment lost, stolen or damaged

Administration fee and hire charges for days vehicle off road due to damage or loss
Costs of any debt recovery agency
Any court and legal costs incurred

Check on daily basis: engine oil level, water level in radiator, washers and wipers, lights, wheel nuts, brake fluid level, tread depth and inflation on all tyres
Drive with reasonable skill and care in accordance with applicable road use rules including Highway Code
Make vehicle available for inspection, service or repair work on reasonable notice

mileage.notes_for_bhr: Fair use mileage cap is tighter than major operators (which typically offer unlimited). Excess mileage charges are an additional cost that could inflate apparent BHR if not identified.

insurance_information_sharing.policy: Motor insurers and their agents share information to prevent fraudulent claims and assess whether insurance cover can be offered. Registers may be searched, and in the event of a claim the information supplied on the form and the accident report form may be put on a register and made available to others. Insurers and their agents reserve the right to confirm licence details with DVLA.

finance_house_rights: Right to visit or enter hirer's place of business to ascertain whereabouts of goods funded by them
Right to uplift goods if hirer is in payment arrears or in breach of any agreement
In event of administration, receivership or liquidation where Finance House not paid: absolute and immediate right to enter premises to identify whereabouts and uplift goods
Finance House may at its complete discretion continue direct relationship with hirer to end of contracted period
If AMT enters administration/liquidation/receivership, hirer shall make rental payments to Finance House directly to avoid termination

governing_law.scotland: If agreement entered into in Scotland, governed by law of Scotland
governing_law.non_contractual_disputes: Also governed by law of England and Wales (or Scotland if entered into in Scotland)

data_protection.sharing: May share with Group companies, insurers, law enforcement, regulatory bodies, credit reference agencies, DVLA, and third parties as permitted under Data Protection Act 2018
data_protection.international_transfers: Personal data may be transferred to countries outside EEA (including USA and India) which do not provide same standard of data protection

rate_inflators_to_identify: Excess reduction / excess waiver — AMT charges separately for reducing the excess. Amount shown on tariff at office. Must be identified and stripped from BHR if credit hire rate includes zero-excess protection.
Young driver additional excess (£400 for age 23-24) — legitimate add-on, but verify claimant's age.
Fuel charges — not part of BHR.
Administration fees for fines, congestion charges etc — not part of BHR.

Mileage excess charges — AMT imposes a fair use cap of 300 miles/day or 1,000/week. Excess mileage rates from tariff. Major operators typically offer unlimited mileage — this is a material difference for BHR comparison.
Additional driver — requires separate insurance proposal form and AMT approval. Not part of base rate.
key_differences_from_major_operators: AMT is an independent operator with 4 UK locations (Leeds, London, Birmingham, Edinburgh) vs hundreds for Hertz/Enterprise/Europcar.
Maximum hire period 84 days vs 28 days (Budget/Avis) or unlimited (most others).
Fair use mileage cap (300 miles/day) vs unlimited mileage at most major operators.
Insurance is AMT's own policy, not a third-party underwriter product — terms may differ from mainstream CDW/SCDW.
Extensive excluded occupation list may affect eligibility of some hirers.
Telematics fitted as standard — vehicle tracking and mileage monitoring.
Accident history check (max 2 accidents in 3 years, or 1 over £5,000) — more restrictive than major operators.
Finance house step-in rights clause suggests vehicles may be financed — relevant to understanding AMT's cost base.
bhr_analysis_notes.what_defendants_argue: AMT is a smaller independent operator whose rates are not directly comparable to major BHR providers. AMT's mileage caps, telematics, and restrictive eligibility criteria distinguish it from mainstream rental. If used as a BHR comparator, the comparison must account for these material differences.
bhr_analysis_notes.what_claimants_argue: AMT is a legitimate BVRLA-member rental company operating from multiple UK locations. Its rates represent genuine market rates available to retail hirers. The mileage cap and other restrictions are standard commercial terms that do not invalidate the rate as a BHR comparator.
bhr_analysis_notes.key_case_law_cross_references: Knowledge base articles 103 (AMT terms), 134 (AMT supplier profile); general BHR case law: Stevens v Equity, Clark v Ardington.

versions.changes_vs_previous_version: Major pricing overhaul. Standard excess increased to £1,500 (was £1,000). Excess waiver now tiered: £15/day for cars, £20/day for 9-seaters/executive/vans (was flat £15/day). Cancellation notice extended to 3 days (was 48 hours) with fee increased to £50 (was £40). Refund guarantee doubled to £10 (was £5). Airport security charge increased to £20 (was £15). Dash cam added as new optional extra at £5/hire. Additional drivers max increased to 4 (was 3). Child seats increased to £5/day (was £1/day) by March 2026. Standard deposit changed to 'up to £150' (was £100). NI licence now supports DVNI code system (previously stated could not generate DVLA code). Sydenham branch added (42 branches, up from 41). Phone number changed to 0141 849 7170 (was 0141 237 4374).
bhr_analysis_notes: Arnold Clark is Europe's largest independent car dealer group — primarily a car dealership that also operates a rental arm. Not a dedicated rental-only company like Enterprise or Hertz.
Base rate is genuinely all-inclusive: VAT, insurance with £1,500 excess, breakdown recovery and mileage (250/day or unlimited for 4+ days) are all included.
The £1,500 standard excess is mid-range — higher than Enterprise (£750) but lower than some others. Excess waiver at £15/day is competitively priced.
Insurance is Arnold Clark's own (not a third-party product), so the excess and waiver terms are set internally.
No upper age limit is a notable feature — many competitors cap at 75 or impose restrictions.
Young driver surcharge of £25/day (under 25) is competitive compared to Avis (£40/day).

Mileage structure is generous for short hires (250 miles/day free) and very generous for 4+ day hires (unlimited). For BHR comparison, this means mileage charges rarely apply in credit hire scenarios.
Travel restrictions are significant: no European travel except NI and Republic of Ireland. This limits utility for cross-border hires.

42 branch locations with heavy concentration in Scotland (reflecting Glasgow HQ). English coverage is thinner — no London branches, limited south of England presence. Southampton is the only southern England location outside airports.
Airport coverage includes Heathrow but not Gatwick, Stansted, Luton, Bristol, Birmingham or East Midlands — notably weaker airport network than Avis/Hertz/Enterprise.
Dual control hire and luxury car hire are separate product lines with different terms — should not be mixed into standard BHR analysis.
One-way hire available but at additional cost — relevant if credit hire claim involves different collection and return locations.
Payment methods are modern (Apple Pay, Google Pay, PayPal) but no cash accepted — all transactions are traceable.

For BHR rebuttal: Arnold Clark's relatively thin English branch network (especially in London and the south) means it is less likely to be a comparable for claimants in those areas. Strongest comparator for Scotland-based claims.

Version history: V1 (Oct-Dec 2022) had free sat nav, £12 additional drivers, 80+ medical requirement, £1,000 excess, 39 branches. V2 (Jan-Jun 2023) introduced sat nav £10, additional drivers £15, added Heathrow and Edinburgh Airport. V3 (Jul 2023-Oct 2024) removed upper age limit, added EU licence restrictions, raised airport security to £15. V4 (Nov 2024-present) is the current major overhaul with £1,500 excess, 3-day cancellation, £50 fee, £10 refund guarantee, £20 airport security, dash cam, 4 additional drivers, and later sub-changes to child seats (£5), deposits (£150), NI DVNI codes, and Sydenham branch.

bhr_analysis_notes: Avis Prestige is a PREMIUM/LUXURY division — NOT standard Avis. It operates under the same company (Avis Budget UK Limited, 00802486) but has entirely separate terms, pricing, and locations.
This is NOT a BHR comparator for standard vehicle categories. It is only relevant where the credit hire claim involves a prestige/luxury vehicle (e.g. Range Rover, Mercedes, BMW, Porsche, Tesla).
Bookings are by specific make and model (not vehicle category/group) — fundamentally different from standard rental where you book a group and get whatever is available.
Minimum age is 25-30 (vehicle dependent) with a maximum of 75. This is more restrictive than standard Avis (23+, no stated upper limit in standard terms).
Excess ranges from £1,000 to £5,000 depending on vehicle — significantly higher than standard Avis. Zero excess option exists but at a daily premium.
Deposit of £1,000-£5,000 required via credit card (no debit cards). Most vehicles require TWO credit cards. This is a very high barrier compared to standard rental.
First additional driver is free — unusual and generous for prestige rental.
Fuel is NOT included (unlike some prestige services). Refuelling charge is £3.20/litre — a significant premium over pump prices.
Mileage is generally unlimited except for Porsches (100 miles/day, £1-2/mile excess). This is important — excess mileage on a Porsche at £2/mile is a significant cost.
Foreign travel requires written authorisation and incurs 35% surcharge plus 100 mile/day mileage cap plus additional insurance. Effectively designed to discourage it.
£200 no-smoking valeting fee is harsh but standard for prestige vehicles.

For BHR rebuttal on prestige vehicle claims: Avis Prestige rates are the legitimate market rate for prestige hire. If a claimant was in (say) a Range Rover Sport, Avis Prestige's rate for that specific vehicle is a valid BHR comparator. However, the high deposit requirement (potentially £5,000 on two credit cards) is relevant to impecuniosity arguments.
The 'on request' booking model (not instant) means availability is not guaranteed — relevant if defendant argues claimant should have used this service instead of credit hire.
versions.changes: Mileage-restricted vehicles changed to Porsche-only (911 Carrera, Cayman 718 GTS, Boxster 718). Zero Excess Option introduced. Digital licences rejected. 28-day mileage note added.

Also offers E-Mobility, Mobility Store, car hire franchise, business car hire, wedding car hire and Drivalia Premier (save 20%). Has a Drivalia Rewards app. Operates meet and greet service at airports (requires 48 hours online check-in).

bhr_analysis_notes: Drivalia (formerly Goldcar/Interrent UK) is part of CA Auto Bank — a European banking group. This gives it a different financial structure from traditional rental companies.

Standard mileage allowance is only 100 miles per day (with 30p/mile excess) — significantly less generous than Avis (unlimited), Arnold Clark (250/day or unlimited 4+ days), or Enterprise. This is critical for BHR comparison: a credit hire claimant using even moderate daily mileage will incur significant excess mileage charges with Drivalia.

Standard excess of £1,500-£2,500 depending on vehicle group is at the higher end. The tiered protection package system is complex — ranging from £0/day (basic, up to £2,500 excess) to £51.99/day (Super Peace of Mind with zero excess, unlimited miles, roadside, loss of rental). For BHR, the relevant rate is the base rate with basic protection.
The 'included in base rate' is somewhat misleading — CDW, TP and FTW are nominally included but with very high excess. The real cost of usable cover requires adding a protection package.

Young driver terms are more permissive than many competitors — they accept 21-22 year olds (with insurance referral and £40/day surcharge) whereas many only start at 23 or 25. However, 21-22 year olds are limited to Cat A and B, no vans, no overseas travel.

Local hirer requirements are notably strict: proof of employment, credit checks, address checks, Category A-D vehicles only with telematics. This creates barriers that may be relevant to impecuniosity arguments.
Maximum hire duration of 90 days in any 12-month period is an insurance-driven restriction that could affect long-duration credit hire claims.

Overseas travel terms are expensive: £40/day green card surcharge, £200+VAT one-off fee, 100 miles/day limit with £0.50/mile excess, plus mandatory 5-star breakdown cover. Effectively prohibitive for casual cross-border travel.
Dash cameras fitted in some vehicles with automatic RTA footage capture — this is unique among rental companies and potentially very useful for liability determination in credit hire disputes.
Telematics fitted in a percentage of fleet — GPS tracking data could be relevant to mileage disputes.

For BHR rebuttal: Drivalia's low mileage allowance (100/day) means their base rate is NOT directly comparable to competitors offering unlimited mileage. A fair comparison must add the unlimited mileage upgrade cost, or account for likely excess mileage charges. This could work either for or against a BHR argument depending on the claimant's actual usage.
The £80 admin fee per damage incident (on top of excess) and £60 per traffic offence are notable additional costs that affect total cost of hire comparisons.
Fuel policy charges (£3/litre + £22.50 admin) are punitive compared to some competitors.

key_barriers_to_retail_hire: EV-only fleet - claimant must need/want an EV specifically
Limited collection points (4 locations: Kent, Devon, Yorkshire, Durham) - significant geographic barrier
High deposit requirements: 2000 with EVision insurance, 500-2000 with own insurance
Strict licence requirements: 5 years for premium EVs, 3 years for standard - excludes newer drivers
No more than 6 points on licence; no DR/IN/TT endorsements; no bans in last 5 years
MasterCard/Visa only - no Amex or Diners Club
Tesla Supercharging no longer included from June 2025 - additional running cost
Connected car tracking on all vehicles - privacy concern for some hirers
Comprehensive own insurance must name EVision/H.E. Group as interested party
500+VAT replacement charge for lost/damaged charging cable
Only 150 miles/day for hires under 12 months - restrictive for long commuters
bhr_rebuttal_points: Specialist EV-only provider - not comparable to mainstream rental market
Very limited geographic coverage (4 sites in England only) - claimant unlikely to have convenient access
2000 deposit is a major financial barrier - many claimants cannot afford this upfront
Tiered cancellation policy penalises claimants heavily (100% within 24hrs, 50% within 48hrs)
29-minute grace period is relatively short - additional day charges can escalate costs
Excess mileage at 0.20+VAT/mile adds up quickly for claimants with longer commutes
Requirement for fully comprehensive insurance naming EVision as interested party is unusual and burdensome
H.E. Group Ltd is primarily a plant hire company - rental car operation is secondary business
No hire and reward insurance available - cannot be used for taxi/PHV replacement
Foreign travel requires written permission + 50+VAT - limits flexibility
30+VAT charge if vehicle not returned fully charged - additional hidden cost
EV range anxiety and charging infrastructure limitations add practical barriers beyond T&Cs
bhr_analysis_notes.overall_assessment: EVision is a niche EV-only rental provider with very limited geographic reach (4 collection points), high deposit requirements, and strict eligibility criteria. As a specialist provider trading under a plant hire parent company, it represents a poor comparator for BHR purposes. The combination of EV-specific barriers (charging costs, range limitations, cable liability), geographic limitations, high deposits, and strict licence requirements make it very difficult for defendants to argue a claimant could reasonably have hired from EVision at retail rates.

Dream Collection requires passport + 2 credit cards + landline + mobile number
Late return charge of GBP 10 plus full additional day charges after 29-min grace
bhr_rebuttal_points: Northern Ireland-specific minimum age of 25 (higher than many UK mainland providers) creates significant barrier for younger claimants
Premium vehicle groups require minimum age 30 - even more restrictive
Young driver surcharge of 30.84/day is one of the highest in the industry
CDW excess of 1,500-2,000 is a major upfront financial risk without Super Cover
Super Cover adds 638-907/month to hire cost - makes retail rates significantly higher than headline rate
Even with Super Cover, wheels/tyres/glass excluded - claimant still at risk of significant charges
Vehicles restricted to island of Ireland only - no European travel whatsoever
Cash rental restrictions are severe: limited groups, office hours only, minimum 550 deposit, no additional drivers
NI licence holders must set up nidirect account to obtain check code - administrative burden
29-minute grace period and strict late return charges penalise claimants who may have unpredictable schedules
70 miles/day cap on 28+ day hires is restrictive for claimants with longer commutes
No-show charge of GBP 95 and late cancellation of GBP 30 add financial risk
Dream Collection and premium vehicles have enhanced security requirements (passport, 2 credit cards)
Hertz Europe Service Centre is in Dublin - complaints/admin for NI customers routed through Republic
bhr_analysis_notes.overall_assessment: Hertz Northern Ireland operates under distinct NI-specific age requirements (min 25, premium 30) with a comprehensive but expensive tiered insurance/waiver structure. The combination of high CDW excesses (1,500-2,000), expensive Super Cover (22-32/day), vehicle travel restricted to the island of Ireland, young driver surcharges (30.84/day), and the NI-specific administrative burden of obtaining check codes via nidirect make it challenging for defendants to argue claimants could easily access retail Hertz rates. The extensive exclusions from even the top-tier Super Cover (wheels, tyres, glass) mean the headline rate never reflects true cost of hire.

barriers_to_retail_hire: CASH NO LONGER ACCEPTED - since policy change, UK rentals require credit or debit card only. Major barrier for unbanked/cash-reliant claimants.
Minimum deposit £200 on credit/debit card (£750 for Dollar/Thrifty brands without SuperCover) - significant preauthorisation hold on card, up to 30 days to release.

Minimum age 25 for standard vehicles, up to 35 for Ford Transit 17-seat (M4 group). Drivers aged 21-24 face young driver surcharge of £47.26/day (£330.76/week, max £1,323.00) - substantially higher than Hertz NI.
Extremely restrictive endorsement policy - DD/DR/UT/IN endorsements within 5 years means outright refusal. Maximum 6 points on specified endorsement types.
CDW non-waivable excess of £1,575 to £5,250 depending on vehicle group creates substantial financial exposure per incident.
If CDW declined, customer exposed to full vehicle value deductibles of £24,000 to £66,090 for EVs.
Additional driver fee of £18.90/day (£132.30/week, £189.00 max) adds significant cost for households needing multiple drivers.
Child/booster/infant seats all charged at £15.76/day (£110.26/week, £157.50 max) - expensive for families needing multiple seats.
NO European travel permitted from UK since January 2021. Republic of Ireland travel requires express permission, VE103B certificate (£171.99-£301.73), and one-way fee.
28+ day hires limited to 70 miles/day with excess at £0.18/mile - restrictive for longer credit hire periods.
No sub-rental, no hire and reward use (standard terms) - vehicle cannot be used as taxi or for car sharing.
SuperCover excludes Dream Collection vehicles, commercial vans, accessories, removable parts, and not available for business contract rates.
Personal Insurance (PI) NOT available in UK - cannot purchase personal injury/effects cover.
Glass & Tyres only available as package with PERS - cannot purchase standalone.
Delivery/collection available but not bookable online and limited to 10-mile radius of branch.
Smoking prohibited - smoking charge applied. Extra cleaning charge if vehicle needs more than standard cleaning.
Damage admin fee of £45 per incident (not covered by CDW/TP, only by SuperCover).
Vehicle must be returned every 28 days for service checks on longer hires - replacement vehicle not guaranteed to be same make/model.
Early return charge possible plus recalculation at current rates which may increase overall cost.
Out of hours returns: remain fully responsible for vehicle including damage until checked in during opening hours.
bhr_rebuttal_arguments: The elimination of cash payments is a fundamental barrier demonstrating that Hertz UK cannot be considered a universally accessible retail option. Many accident victims, particularly elderly or financially vulnerable claimants, may not have credit/debit cards suitable for rental deposits.

The deposit preauthorisation of £200-£750 represents a significant financial burden beyond the rental cost itself, with funds tied up for up to 30 days post-rental. This is a real cost to the claimant that BHR calculations typically ignore.
Young driver surcharges of up to £47.26/day (£1,323 max) represent one of the highest in the market and would substantially inflate any BHR calculation for claimants aged 21-24.
The age minimum of 25 (standard) up to 35 for certain groups means younger claimants face either refusal or punitive surcharges that are not reflected in simple BHR rate comparisons.

CDW excess exposure of £1,575-£5,250 per incident without SuperCover represents a material risk that credit hire companies absorb but retail hirers must either accept or pay additional premiums to eliminate.
The complete ban on European travel from UK since January 2021 demonstrates that Hertz UK vehicles are geographically restricted in ways that credit hire vehicles may not be.
The 70 miles/day cap on 28+ day hires at £0.18p excess penalises longer-term hirers (typical of credit hire durations) who need to drive normal daily distances.

Hertz URP/PCO terms demonstrate that hire and reward use requires completely separate terms, dedicated insurance (Hire & Reward cover with £10m public liability), PHV licensing, and lower excesses (£1,000 own damage) - proving that standard rental terms are fundamentally incompatible with commercial use.

The URP terms also show that even Hertz's own private hire programme has restrictions not applicable to credit hire: no additional drivers at all, mandatory dashcams, vehicle grounding capability, mandatory MOTs/inspections at Hertz-approved centres, territory restrictions tied to Uber allocation.

The prohibition on carrying passengers for remuneration under standard terms means BHR rates cannot be compared to credit hire rates where the hire company provides a full replacement vehicle service - these are fundamentally different products.

SuperCover exclusions (Dream Collection, commercial vans, accessories, removable parts) mean the insurance product available to retail hirers is more limited than the comprehensive cover typically included in credit hire agreements.
The mandatory 28-day service check return requirement for longer rentals creates operational disruption not experienced with credit hire, where the hire company manages fleet maintenance.
Delivery/collection restricted to 10-mile radius of branch and not bookable online - significant inconvenience compared to credit hire companies who typically deliver to claimant's home or workplace.
The £1,980 repatriation charge for unauthorised foreign travel demonstrates the severity of geographic restrictions and the financial penalties that retail hirers face for terms violations.

barriers_to_retail_hire: LUXURY/PRESTIGE ONLY FLEET - No standard or economy vehicles at all. Cheapest vehicle is £245+VAT/day (£294 inc VAT). Fleet consists entirely of supercars, sports cars, and luxury SUVs.

EXTREMELY HIGH DAILY RATES - Prices range from £245+VAT to £1,295+VAT per day. These are not comparable to mainstream hire rates and would never form part of a reasonable BHR calculation for standard vehicle replacement.

bhr_rebuttal_arguments: PCH is fundamentally NOT a like-for-like replacement hire option for mainstream vehicles. It is a luxury experience company catering to supercar enthusiasts, weddings, TV/film, and events. Including PCH rates in any BHR calculation would be comparing apples to oranges.

The cheapest vehicle in the fleet (Audi Q7/A8/Land Rover Discovery at £245+VAT/day = £294 inc VAT) costs more per day than many mainstream companies charge per WEEK for a standard family car. This demonstrates that prestige hire is a completely different market segment.

The 25% non-refundable deposit represents a significant financial commitment that retail hirers must accept upfront, with no guarantee of refund if circumstances change. This is far more onerous than mainstream rental deposits which are typically refundable preauthorisations.

The absence of any CDW or excess waiver product means customers face the full insurance excess on vehicles worth £50,000-£300,000+. The financial exposure from a single incident could be devastating for a retail hirer, whereas credit hire companies absorb this risk entirely.
The enquiry-based booking process (no self-service online booking) creates delays and uncertainty in vehicle availability, unlike mainstream rental companies where instant online booking is standard.

PCH's fleet and pricing structure proves that prestige vehicle replacement is a specialist market with fundamentally different economics to standard car hire. BHR calculations based on mainstream rental rates cannot account for the true cost of replacing a prestige vehicle.
The £1.50/mile excess mileage charge is significantly higher than mainstream companies (typically £0.10-£0.25/mile), reflecting the higher running costs and depreciation of prestige vehicles.
The requirement that only the hirer can make payment (no third party payments) creates additional barriers for credit hire claimants whose hire is being funded by the at-fault insurer.

PCH's membership of BVRLA and LNQA certification confirms it operates as a legitimate rental company, but its specialist nature means it serves a fundamentally different market to the mainstream rental companies typically used in BHR comparisons.

MINIMUM AGE 25: Age restriction of 25 minimum (potentially higher per vehicle) excludes younger drivers
NO EXCESS REDUCTION: No CDW or excess waiver product available - hirer fully exposed to excess per incident equal to deposit amount
CASH NOT ACCEPTED: No cash payments (except settling at Bayswater) - credit/debit card mandatory with deposit frozen
NO EARLY RETURN REFUNDS: Absolutely no refunds on early terminations - customer locked into full hire cost regardless
48HR ADVANCE PAYMENT: Full payment taken 48 hours before hire begins - significant upfront financial commitment
MAXIMUM 90 DAY HIRE: Hire capped at 90 days maximum
NO OVERSEAS WITHOUT PERMISSION: Vehicle cannot leave UK without written permission and additional fees
LOSS OF USE CHARGES: Pro-rata daily rate charged for each day vehicle unavailable after incident - significant financial exposure on luxury vehicle daily rates
GROSS NEGLIGENCE EXCLUSIONS: Extensive list of gross negligence circumstances voiding insurance (overhead strikes, flooding, wrong fuel, warning lights etc)
DEPOSIT FROZEN POST-ACCIDENT: Security deposit frozen after any accident until confirmed no claim - funds tied up indefinitely
24HR ACCIDENT REPORTING: Failure to report accident within 24 hours may invalidate insurance entirely
CHRISTMAS NON-CANCELLABLE: 15-31 December bookings cannot be cancelled at all
SMOKING CHARGE £500: Up to £500 charge for smoking in vehicle
FUEL PENALTY 2.5X: Fuel returned below collection level charged at 2.5 times local pump rate
HIRE AND REWARD PROHIBITED: No hire and reward use without written permission and own insurance
bhr_analysis.bhr_rebuttal_notes: Prestige Collection is a luxury/prestige-only rental operation with just 4 UK locations (only 1 own premises), operated by City Group Inter-Rent Ltd in partnership with Europcar. It is wholly unsuitable as a BHR comparator for standard credit hire rates. The fleet consists exclusively of high-end luxury vehicles at premium pricing - this is not a mainstream rental supplier. With only 4 locations (3 being Europcar concessions), no delivery service, restricted hours, no excess reduction products, mandatory 48hr advance payment, no early return refunds, and extensive insurance exclusions including gross negligence circumstances and 24hr reporting requirements, this supplier presents overwhelming barriers to retail hire for ordinary motorists. The loss-of-use charges at luxury daily rates and deposit freezing post-accident create extraordinary financial risk. This is a niche luxury operator catering to a specific affluent market segment and cannot reasonably be compared to standard vehicle group hire rates.

barriers_to_retail_hire: FRANCHISE OPERATION: Downeys Rental Ltd is a franchisee of Sixt GmbH operating exclusively in NI - not directly comparable to Sixt UK mainland operations
MANDATORY LDW: Loss Damage Waiver is mandatory in NI unlike mainland UK, making base rates inherently different
CREDIT CARD REQUIREMENT: Credit card mandatory for booking. Debit cards severely restricted - premium/luxury groups not permitted on debit at all
HIGH DEPOSITS: Credit card deposits £600-£2,000 by group. Without LDW deposits £3,000-£10,000. Debit card deposits immediately debited from bank account
STRICT LICENCE REQUIREMENTS: 3+ years licence required. Under 25 must be endorsement-free. Over 25 max 6 points. No disqualification within 5 years
EXTENSIVE PROHIBITED ENDORSEMENTS: 13 specific endorsement codes prohibited (AC, BA, TT, NE, CD, IN, LC, MS, UT, DD, DG, DR, XX)
EXCLUDED OCCUPATIONS: Wide range of occupations excluded including rally drivers, traders, scrap dealers, professional gamblers, sports professionals, entertainment, students, unemployed
EXCLUDED HISTORY: 2+ accidents in 3 years or insurance previously cancelled/declined/refused bars rental
AGE RESTRICTIONS: Complex age/group matrix - under 25 restricted to smallest groups. Over 30 required for premium. 35+ for XXAR. Max age 75
NON-UK RESIDENTS: Minimum age 25 for all groups regardless
LIMITED TERRITORY: NI only by default. ROI requires written permission + £24/day charge. £500 fine for leaving island without permission
NO EARLY RETURN REFUNDS: Full rental charge payable even if returned early
MAXIMUM 84 DAYS: Cannot extend beyond 84 days - must return vehicle and start new contract
BOOKING CHANGE FEES: £24.99+VAT to change booking. Prepaid cancellation loses first 3 days or entire amount if <3 days before
RESTRICTED PROFESSIONS: Professional sports people, actors, musicians, TV personalities, Royalties face special insurance conditions
NO HIRE OR REWARD: Vehicle cannot be used for hire/reward, racing, competitions, or goods carriage for hire/reward
SINGLE FRANCHISEE TERRITORY: Only one operator in NI - limited branch availability compared to mainland networks
bhr_rebuttal_points: Sixt NI is operated by Downeys Rental Ltd as a franchise - a retail customer dealing with this entity faces the specific terms of a local franchisee, not Sixt's global/UK terms
The mandatory LDW requirement in NI means the base rate is structurally different from mainland UK Sixt rates and cannot be directly compared
Credit card requirement with deposits of £600-£2,000 (or £3,000-£10,000 without LDW) presents a significant financial barrier for many claimants
Debit card restrictions are severe - premium/luxury groups cannot use debit cards at all, and debit deposits are immediately debited rather than held
The 3-year minimum licence requirement combined with endorsement restrictions and occupation exclusions eliminates a significant portion of the driving population
The 13 prohibited endorsement codes and strict points limits (0 for under 25, 6 for over 25) are more restrictive than many competitors
Occupation exclusions covering entertainment, professional sport, students, and unemployed persons are unusually broad
Cross-border charges to ROI (£24/day, written permission only, £500 fine for breach) add significant cost and friction for anyone needing to travel to the Republic
No early return refund policy means full rental charge applies regardless of actual usage period
The complex age/vehicle group matrix with multiple tiers (21/25/30/35) creates uncertainty for younger drivers about vehicle availability
Admin fees for damage (£30-£140) and fines (£45) are additional costs beyond the rental rate
84-day maximum with mandatory new contract requirement creates administrative burden for longer-term needs

barriers_to_retail_hire: CREDIT CARD/DEBIT REQUIREMENT: Credit or debit card mandatory. Debit cards restricted - cannot use for deposits on premium/luxury vehicles unless Super Top Cover purchased

LICENCE REQUIREMENTS: Minimum 1 year licence held. DVLA check code or printed endorsement record mandatory for all approved drivers. Max 8 points (0 for under 24)
NON-UK RESIDENTS: Passport/ID card required valid 3+ months beyond end of rental. Non-EU licences valid for first 12 months from UK entry only. IDP required for non-Roman alphabet licences.
AGE RESTRICTIONS: Complex group-specific minimums (21/23/25/30 for cars, 21/25 for vans). Young driver surcharge for under 28 (cars) / under 25 (vans)
NO EARLY RETURN REFUNDS: Full rental charge payable if collected late or returned early. Prepaid: no refund at all. Non-prepaid: £10 ex VAT early return fee + possible higher standard price
MAXIMUM 84 DAYS: Cannot extend beyond 84 days - must return vehicle and agree new contract with possible new deposit
RESTRICTED PROFESSIONS: Professional sports people, actors, musicians, TV personalities, Royalties face special insurance conditions where Sixt/insurer may recover full costs
NO HIRE OR REWARD: Vehicle cannot be used for hire/reward without prior written consent, no racing, no off-road, no operator's licence use
CROSS-BORDER RESTRICTIONS: Written permission required. Not possible for commercial vehicles, multi-seaters, premium vehicles, premium EVs. Zone 2 countries not permitted at all
PARTNER ORGANISATION REFERRAL: Sixt may pass reservation to another business at their discretion - terms may change, deposits may differ
CANCELLATION COSTS: Prepaid cancellation charge = rental charge for max 3 days. £99 cancellation fee. No-show fee £69
DAMAGE LIABILITY: Extensive damage matrix system. Admin fees £20-£125 per claim. Specific charges: stone chip £60 + admin, rim scratch £120 + admin
REPOSSESSION RIGHTS: May repossess within 2 hours of rental expiry. Customer grants permission to access premises for repossession
CLEANING CHARGES: £75 for condition issues (odours, pet hair, bodily fluids, etc.). Additional charges for smoking.
FUEL SURCHARGES: 160% surcharge on non-prepaid fuel rate. 10% on prepaid. EV must return at 80%+ charge or be charged per kWh.
TELEMETRY AND MONITORING: Vehicles fitted with telemetry tracking speed, location. May remotely immobilise if believed stolen.
THIRD PARTY COVER LIMITATIONS: Does not cover injury to driver or personal items inside vehicle
bhr_rebuttal_points: Sixt UK requires credit/debit card with deposits of £300-£750 immediately tied up, presenting financial barriers for many claimants who may not have available credit
The complex age/vehicle group matrix (21/23/25/30 minimum ages across different groups) means many younger claimants face restricted vehicle availability or young driver surcharges
Maximum 8 points licence restriction plus zero endorsements for under-24s excludes a significant portion of drivers
Non-UK residents face multiple additional requirements (passport validity, IDP, 12-month licence limitation) creating barriers for foreign national claimants
Restricted profession provisions mean Sixt/insurer can seek full cost recovery from sports professionals, actors, musicians, TV personalities - making rental economically risky for these groups
No early return refunds plus £10 fee for unannounced early return means claimants pay for the full booked period regardless of when their own vehicle is repaired
The £75 cleaning charge threshold is strict (pet hairs, odours, sand, stubborn stains, bodily fluids) creating risk of additional charges
Cross-border restrictions prohibiting commercial vehicles, premium cars, premium EVs, and multi-seaters from European travel significantly limits utility
Partner Organisation referral clause means Sixt may redirect the booking to a completely different company with different terms and deposits
Fuel surcharge of 160% on non-prepaid rate is one of the highest in the industry, creating significant penalty for not returning with full tank
84-day maximum rental period with mandatory new contract creates administrative and financial burden for longer-term replacement vehicle needs
Damage admin fees (£20-£125) plus specific fixed charges (£60 stone chip, £120 rim scratch) are additional costs beyond repair that a retail hirer must absorb
The Digital Rental (Sixt App) appendix modifies many standard protections - customers using the app face different terms including modified inspection procedures
Telemetry tracking of vehicle speed and location, with power to remotely immobilise, represents unusual surveillance conditions for a retail hire

Based in Essex (Brentwood) with limited geographic reach — not a national provider.
No refund policy even if bike returned early — punitive for claimants whose repair completes sooner than expected.
Chain maintenance responsibility on hirer during the hire period — unusual and burdensome requirement.
Endorsement restrictions (max 6 points, no DR/TN/BA/UT/CD codes) may exclude many motorcycle riders.

For BHR rebuttal on motorcycle claims: Superbike Rental rates are a legitimate specialist comparator but the high deposit, minimum age, minimum hire period, and geographic limitations significantly reduce accessibility for the average claimant.

bhr_analysis_notes: Questor is NOT a rental company — it is a car hire excess insurance broker. It does not provide vehicles.
Relevant to BHR because defendants may argue: mainstream rental (e.g. Avis at £X/day) + Questor excess insurance (e.g. £7-£10/day) = cheaper alternative to zero-excess credit hire.
The cost of a Questor policy added to a mainstream BHR provides a like-for-like comparison to credit hire rates that include zero excess.

Key limitation: Questor reimburses AFTER the event — the renter must still pay the excess to the rental company first and then claim it back. This is fundamentally different from credit hire zero-excess which means the claimant never pays.
Vehicle value cap (£100,000) and age cap (20 years) may exclude some prestige/classic vehicles.
Personal effects cover is minimal (£75 per item, £200 max) — not comparable to credit hire companies that may include more comprehensive cover.
Claims must be reported within 30 days — a claimant unfamiliar with the process could miss the deadline.
Single trip policies cover up to 180 days — adequate for most credit hire durations.
The 'Questor argument' is strongest when the claimant's vehicle is a standard category where mainstream rental + Questor would produce a significantly lower total than the credit hire rate.
Counter-argument: requiring an impecunious claimant to pay the excess upfront and wait for reimbursement defeats the purpose of credit hire. The claimant should not have to act as their own insurer.

key_barriers_to_retail_hire: EV-ONLY FLEET: Claimant's damaged vehicle is almost certainly an ICE vehicle — being offered an EV replacement raises practical issues (charging infrastructure, range anxiety, unfamiliarity with EV operation)
SINGLE LOCATION: Operates from one site at 3 Durham St, Portsmouth PO1 1NA — no branch network whatsoever
REGIONAL SUPPLIER: Despite claiming nationwide delivery, Voltage is a regional operator in the Portsmouth/Southampton area. Delivery to distant locations is at additional cost to the hirer
MINIMUM 3-DAY HIRE: Cannot hire for shorter periods — inflexible for short repair durations
TESLA MINIMUM 7-DAY HIRE: Model X requires minimum 7-day hire — even more restrictive
AGE RESTRICTIONS: Minimum age 25 for standard vehicles, 30 for Teslas/IONIQ 5 — excludes drivers aged 21-24 (and 25-29 for premium vehicles)
LICENCE REQUIREMENTS: Minimum 2 years clean licence for standard vehicles, 5 years for Teslas — excludes newer drivers
POINTS NOT GUARANTEED: 6 points maximum but acceptance subject to insurer discretion — no certainty of hire even if points are within limit
CHARGING RESPONSIBILITY: Hirer must charge the vehicle at own expense — this is an additional running cost not reflected in the daily rate and not applicable to ICE vehicle hire
50% CHARGE RETURN REQUIREMENT: Vehicle must be returned with at least 50% charge — failure to do so presumably incurs a penalty charge
TESLA CHILL MODE: All Teslas restricted to Chill Mode — reduced performance compared to what a retail customer would expect from these vehicles
PRICES EXCLUDE INSURANCE AND VAT: Published daily rates are net of insurance and VAT — true cost to a retail hirer is significantly higher

HIGH EXCESS LIABILITY: Standard excess £1,000 (reducible to £500 with CDW); Tesla Model 3 excess £2,000 (reducible to £1,000); Tesla Model Y/X excess £4,000 (reducible to £2,000). Even with CDW, the hirer retains significant financial exposure
bhr_rebuttal_points: REGIONAL NOT NATIONAL: Voltage operates from a single address in Portsmouth. Offering paid delivery does NOT make them a national supplier — by that logic, any company that posts items is 'national'. They are a specialist regional EV hire company.

EV-ONLY IS NOT EQUIVALENT: Unless the claimant's damaged vehicle was an EV, offering an EV replacement is not like-for-like. The claimant would need access to charging infrastructure, may not have a home charger, and faces range limitations not present with their ICE vehicle.
HIDDEN COSTS: Published rates EXCLUDE insurance and VAT. Adding comprehensive insurance + 20% VAT to the daily rate significantly increases the true cost. Charging costs are also additional.
SMALL SPECIALIST OPERATOR: Voltage is a micro-business operating from a single site. Their fleet size is necessarily limited — availability for any given vehicle at any given date cannot be assumed.
DELIVERY COST NOT IN RATE: If delivery is cited for a distant claimant, the delivery/collection charge is an ADDITIONAL cost that should be added to the BHR daily rate comparison.

NO BRANCH NETWORK: Unlike mainstream rental companies, there is no branch the claimant can attend. If the vehicle breaks down or has issues, the claimant is reliant on a single operator 500 miles away (if distant delivery was used).

IMPRACTICALITY FOR MOST CLAIMANTS: The combination of EV-only fleet, single location, minimum hire periods, age restrictions, licence requirements, and charging responsibilities make Voltage impractical for the vast majority of credit hire claimants.

EXCESS EXPOSURE: Even with CDW purchased (at additional daily cost), the hirer faces £500-£2,000 excess. Without CDW, excess ranges from £1,000 to £4,000 — a substantial upfront financial risk that credit hire claimants do not bear.
bhr_analysis_notes.overall_assessment: Voltage (trading as Volt-Age) is a small, specialist EV-only hire company operating from a single location in Portsmouth. Despite claims of nationwide delivery capability, they are fundamentally a regional supplier. Their published rates exclude insurance and VAT, making headline comparisons misleading. The EV-only fleet creates practical barriers for most claimants (charging infrastructure, range, unfamiliarity), and strict eligibility requirements (age 25-30, clean licence 2-5 years, points at insurer discretion) exclude a significant proportion of the driving population. For BHR purposes, Voltage is a poor comparator: they are not a mainstream rental company, have no branch network, offer only EVs (which are not equivalent to most claimants' ICE vehicles), and their true cost including insurance, VAT, and charging significantly exceeds the published daily rate.

bhr_analysis_notes: CRITICAL DISTINCTION: Enterprise's Damage Waiver (DW) is included in ALL standard UK rates as standard — not 'often included' or conditional. This is fundamentally different from Hertz, Europcar, Sixt and Budget where CDW/TP inclusion is conditional and must be verified on the rental agreement.
DW excess is £1,750–£2,250 depending on vehicle type (default £2,000). This is the amount payable per incident even with DW included. EP can reduce this to £100–£250.

Maximum rental period in UK is 88 calendar days — relevant to extended credit hire periods.

Section 8(d)(ii) explicitly provides for credit hire/deferred payment: where rental arises from an accident, payment deferred up to 11 months at no interest. This confirms Enterprise actively participates in the credit hire market.
Pick-up/drop-off service free within 10-mile radius of non-airport branches — relevant when assessing whether a claimant could have accessed a conventional hire vehicle.
Third party liability inclusion depends on whether '3RD PARTY COVER INCLUDED' appears on the individual Rental Agreement Summary — not universally included, unlike Hertz.
29-minute grace period for late returns — same as Hertz.
Fines/offences admin fee capped at £45 — same as Hertz.
Under-25 renters may face vehicle class restrictions and surcharges but specific rates are not published in standard T&Cs — will appear on Rental Agreement Summary.

ENTERPRISE HOLDINGS GROUP (UK): Enterprise, National and Alamo are all part of Enterprise Holdings. In the UK, National and Alamo branches typically share premises, staff and vehicle fleet with co-located Enterprise branches. A BHR survey that cites Enterprise, National and Alamo as three independent comparators is citing the same physical operation under three brand names. The independence of such survey evidence is seriously undermined. Challenge the BHR provider to confirm whether the quoted comparators operate from the same premises, share the same fleet management, and are commercially distinct.

ENTERPRISE HOLDINGS GROUP (UK): Enterprise, National and Alamo are all part of Enterprise Holdings. In the UK, National and Alamo branches typically share premises, staff and vehicle fleet with co-located Enterprise branches. A BHR survey that cites Enterprise, National and Alamo as three independent comparators is citing the same physical operation under three brand names. The independence of such survey evidence is seriously undermined. Challenge the BHR provider to confirm whether the quoted comparators operate from the same premises, share the same fleet management, and are commercially distinct.

lancaster_gate.status_reason: Lancaster Gate is a Credit Hire Organisation (CHO), not a conventional hire company. Rates are GTA-protocol credit hire rates, not basic hire rates. WhichRate deploys Lancaster Gate credit hire agreements as purported evidence of BHR in motorcycle claims where no genuine BHR is available. This must be challenged.
lancaster_gate.notes: CRITICAL: Lancaster Gate is NOT a BHR comparator. It is a credit hire organisation whose rates are ABI-GTA protocol credit hire rates. WhichRate has adopted the tactic of serving a Lancaster Gate credit hire agreement as Exhibit WR/1 in motorcycle claims, asserting these rates are indicative of the BHR. This is fundamentally misconceived: (1) Lancaster Gate is a CHO, not a conventional hire company — its rates are credit hire rates recoverable from the at-fault insurer under the GTA protocol; (2) A credit hire agreement is not evidence of basic hire rates available to a member of the public; (3) The agreement does not evidence the terms and conditions of hire, the elements included in the hire charge, or demonstrate that the rate is available to an impecunious claimant paying out of pocket; (4) The witness admits BHR is unavailable (e.g. Churton statement: 'Basic Hire Rates (BHR) are unavailable within the Claimant's locality') — the Lancaster Gate rate is offered as CHR, not BHR; (5) WhichRate's own witness expressly excludes enquiry into deposits ('My remit does not include enquiries into the financial business processes (i.e deposits) of vehicle providers'). The witness should be put to strict proof as to why they assert a credit hire invoice evidences BHR.

whichrate_tactic.description: WhichRate deploys Lancaster Gate credit hire agreements in motorcycle B2 claims where no BHR providers are available. The witness statement admits BHR is unavailable and labels the Lancaster Gate rate as CHR, yet presents it as the alternative rate the court should adopt.
whichrate_tactic.typical_exhibit: Credit Hire Agreement from Lancaster Gate for ABI-GTA B2 motorcycle
whichrate_tactic.witness: Mark Churton, Rates Surveyor, Whichrate Consulting Ltd
rebuttal_grounds: Lancaster Gate is a CHO, not a conventional hire company — it does not offer vehicles to members of the public at BHR rates
The rates are ABI-GTA protocol credit hire rates, which are set by agreement between CHOs and insurers for subrogated recovery — they do not represent the open market rate
WhichRate's own witness admits BHR is unavailable, then substitutes CHR as if it were BHR — this conflates two fundamentally different rate categories
The credit hire agreement does not evidence the terms and conditions of hire available to a cash-paying member of the public
The agreement does not detail what is included in the daily hire charge (insurance scope, breakdown cover, excess terms, etc.)
The witness expressly excludes enquiry into deposits and financial barriers — the very matters that determine whether a rate is genuinely available to an impecunious claimant
A credit hire agreement evidences what a CHO charges an at-fault insurer, not what a claimant would pay out of pocket
The witness should be put to strict proof: on what basis does a credit hire invoice from a CHO evidence the basic hire rate?

A novice driver — particularly one close to a driving test date — is unlikely to want their instructor to provide lessons in a different type of vehicle. The dual controls alter the driving experience and are not equivalent to a standard rental car for a non-instructor claimant.
